"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"https://zod.dev/api#exclusive-unions-xor\\n\\n## [Exclusive unions (XOR)](https://zod.dev/api?id=exclusive-unions-xor)\\n\\nAn exclusive union (XOR) is a union where exactly one option must match. Unlike regular unions that succeed when any option matches, `z.xor()` fails if zero options match OR if multiple options match.\\n\\n```\\nconst schema = z.xor([z.string(), z.number()]);\\n \\nschema.parse(\\\"hello\\\"); // ✅ passes\\nschema.parse(42);      // ✅ passes\\nschema.parse(true);    // ❌ fails (zero matches)\\n```\\n\\nThis is useful when you want to ensure mutual exclusivity between options:\\n\\n```\\n// Validate that exactly ONE of these matches\\nconst payment = z.xor([\\n  z.object({ type: z.literal(\\\"card\\\"), cardNumber: z.string() }),\\n  z.object({ type: z.literal(\\\"bank\\\"), accountNumber: z.string() }),\\n]);\\n \\npayment.parse({ type: \\\"card\\\", cardNumber: \\\"1234\\\" }); // ✅ passes\\n```\\n\\nIf the input could match multiple options, `z.xor()` will fail:\\n\\n```\\nconst overlapping = z.xor([z.string(), z.any()]);\\noverlapping.parse(\\\"hello\\\"); // ❌ fails (matches both string and any)\\n```\\n\\n\\n\\nhttps://zod.dev/api#discriminated-unions\\n\\n## [Discriminated unions](https://zod.dev/api?id=discriminated-unions)\\n\\nA [discriminated union](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/narrowing.html#discriminated-unions) is a special kind of union in which a) all the options are object schemas that b) share a particular key (the \\\"discriminator\\\"). Based on the value of the discriminator key, TypeScript is able to \\\"narrow\\\" the type signature as you'd expect.\\n\\n```\\ntype MyResult =\\n  | { status: \\\"success\\\"; data: string }\\n  | { status: \\\"failed\\\"; error: string };\\n \\nfunction handleResult(result: MyResult){\\n  if(result.status === \\\"success\\\"){\\n    result.data; // string\\n  } else {\\n    result.error; // string\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nYou could represent it with a regular `z.union()` . But regular unions are *naive* —they check the input against each option in order and return the first one that passes. This can be slow for large unions.\\n\\nSo Zod provides a `z.discriminatedUnion()` API that uses a *discriminator key* to make parsing more efficient.\\n\\n```\\nconst MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), error: z.string() }),\\n]);\\n```\\n\\nEach option should be an *object schema* whose discriminator prop ( `status` in the example above) corresponds to some literal value or set of values, usually `z.enum()` , `z.literal()` , `z.null()` , or `z.undefined()` .\\n\\n###  Nesting discriminated unions\\n\\n For advanced use cases, discriminated unions can be nested. Zod will figure out the optimal parsing strategy to leverage the discriminators at each level.\\n\\n```\\nconst BaseError = { status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), message: z.string() };\\nconst MyErrors = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"code\\\", [\\n  z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(400) }),\\n  z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(401) }),\\n  z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(500) }),\\n]);\\n \\nconst MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n  MyErrors\\n]);\\n```\\n\\n\\n\\nhttps://zod.dev/api#intersections\\n\\n## [Intersections](https://zod.dev/api?id=intersections)\\n\\nIntersection types ( `A & B` ) represent a logical \\\"AND\\\".\\n\\n```\\nconst a = z.union([z.number(), z.string()]);\\nconst b = z.union([z.number(), z.boolean()]);\\nconst c = z.intersection(a, b);\\n \\ntype c = z.infer<typeof c>; // => number\\n```\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Defining schemas\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/api#exclusive-unions-xor\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nYou could represent it with a regular `z.union()` . But regular unions are *naive* —they check the input against each option in order and return the first one that passes. This can be slow for large unions.\\n\\nSo Zod provides a `z.discriminatedUnion()` API that uses a *discriminator key* to make parsing more efficient.\\n\\n```\\nconst MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), error: z.string() }),\\n]);\\n```\\n\\nEach option should be an *object schema* whose discriminator prop ( `status` in the example above) corresponds to some literal value or set of values, usually `z.enum()` , `z.literal()` , `z.null()` , or `z.undefined()` .\\n\\n###  Nesting discriminated unions\\n\\n\\n\\nhttps://zod.dev/api#intersections\\n\\n## [Intersections](https://zod.dev/api?id=intersections)\\n\\nIntersection types ( `A & B` ) represent a logical \\\"AND\\\".\\n\\n```\\nconst a = z.union([z.number(), z.string()]);\\nconst b = z.union([z.number(), z.boolean()]);\\nconst c = z.intersection(a, b);\\n \\ntype c = z.infer<typeof c>; // => number\\n```\\n\\nThis can be useful for intersecting two object types.\\n\\n```\\nconst Person = z.object({ name: z.string() });\\ntype Person = z.infer<typeof Person>;\\n \\nconst Employee = z.object({ role: z.string() });\\ntype Employee = z.infer<typeof Employee>;\\n \\nconst EmployedPerson = z.intersection(Person, Employee);\\ntype EmployedPerson = z.infer<typeof EmployedPerson>;\\n// Person & Employee\\n```\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n   When merging object schemas, prefer [`A.extend(B)`](https://zod.dev/api#extend) over intersections. Using `.extend()` will give you a new object schema, whereas `z.intersection(A, B)` returns a `ZodIntersection` instance which lacks common object methods like `pick` and `omit` .\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Defining schemas\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/api#exclusive-unions-xor\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n### [Custom email regex](https://zod.dev/v4?id=custom-email-regex)\\n\\nThe `z.email()` API now supports a custom regular expression. There is no one canonical email regex; different applications may choose to be more or less strict. For convenience Zod exports some common ones.\\n\\n```\\n// Zod's default email regex (Gmail rules)\\n// see colinhacks.com/essays/reasonable-email-regex\\nz.email(); // z.regexes.email\\n \\n// the regex used by browsers to validate input[type=email] fields\\n// https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/input/email\\nz.email({ pattern: z.regexes.html5Email });\\n \\n// the classic emailregex.com regex (RFC 5322)\\nz.email({ pattern: z.regexes.rfc5322Email });\\n \\n// a loose regex that allows Unicode (good for intl emails)\\nz.email({ pattern: z.regexes.unicodeEmail });\\n```\\n\\n## [Template literal types](https://zod.dev/v4?id=template-literal-types)\\n\\nZod 4 implements `z.templateLiteral()` . Template literal types are perhaps the biggest feature of TypeScript's type system that wasn't previously representable.\\n\\n```\\nconst hello = z.templateLiteral([\\\"hello, \\\", z.string()]);\\n// `hello, ${string}`\\n \\nconst cssUnits = z.enum([\\\"px\\\", \\\"em\\\", \\\"rem\\\", \\\"%\\\"]);\\nconst css = z.templateLiteral([z.number(), cssUnits]);\\n// `${number}px` | `${number}em` | `${number}rem` | `${number}%`\\n \\nconst email = z.templateLiteral([\\n  z.string().min(1),\\n  \\\"@\\\",\\n  z.string().max(64),\\n]);\\n// `${string}@${string}` (the min/max refinements are enforced!)\\n```\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nEvery Zod schema type that can be stringified stores an internal regex: strings, string formats like `z.email()` , numbers, boolean, bigint, enums, literals, undefined/optional, null/nullable, and other template literals. The `z.templateLiteral` constructor concatenates these into a super-regex, so things like string formats ( `z.email()` ) are properly enforced (but custom refinements are not!).\\n\\nRead the [template literal docs](https://zod.dev/api#template-literals) for more info.\\n\\n## [Number formats](https://zod.dev/v4?id=number-formats)\\n\\nNew numeric \\\"formats\\\" have been added for representing fixed-width integer and float types. These return a `ZodNumber` instance with proper inclusive minimum/maximum constraints already added.\\n\\n```\\nz.int();      // [Number.MIN_SAFE_INTEGER, Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER]\\nz.float32();  // [-3.4028234663852886e38, 3.4028234663852886e38]\\nz.float64();  // [-1.7976931348623157e308, 1.7976931348623157e308]\\nz.int32();    // [-2147483648, 2147483647]\\nz.uint32();   // [0, 4294967295]\\n```\\n\\nSimilarly the following `bigint` numeric formats have also been added. These integer types exceed what can be safely represented by a `number` in JavaScript, so these return a `ZodBigInt` instance with the proper inclusive minimum/maximum constraints already added.\\n\\n```\\nz.int64();    // [-9223372036854775808n, 9223372036854775807n]\\nz.uint64();   // [0n, 18446744073709551615n]\\n```\\n\\n## [Stringbool](https://zod.dev/v4?id=stringbool)\\n\\nThe existing `z.coerce.boolean()` API is very simple: falsy values ( `false` , `undefined` , `null` , `0` , `\\\"\\\"` , `NaN` etc) become `false` , truthy values become `true` .\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nThis is still a good API, and its behavior aligns with the other `z.coerce` APIs. But some users requested a more sophisticated \\\"env-style\\\" boolean coercion. To support this, Zod 4 introduces `z.stringbool()` :\\n\\n```\\nconst strbool = z.stringbool();\\n \\nstrbool.parse(\\\"true\\\")         // => true\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"1\\\")            // => true\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"yes\\\")          // => true\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"on\\\")           // => true\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"y\\\")            // => true\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"enabled\\\")      // => true\\n \\nstrbool.parse(\\\"false\\\");       // => false\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"0\\\");           // => false\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"no\\\");          // => false\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"off\\\");         // => false\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"n\\\");           // => false\\nstrbool.parse(\\\"disabled\\\");    // => false\\n \\nstrbool.parse(/* anything else */); // ZodError<[{ code: \\\"invalid_value\\\" }]>\\n```\\n\\nTo customize the truthy and falsy values:\\n\\n```\\nz.stringbool({\\n  truthy: [\\\"yes\\\", \\\"true\\\"],\\n  falsy: [\\\"no\\\", \\\"false\\\"]\\n})\\n```\\n\\nRefer to the [`z.stringbool()` docs](https://zod.dev/api#stringbools) for more information.\\n\\n## [Simplified error customization](https://zod.dev/v4?id=simplified-error-customization)\\n\\nThe majority of breaking changes in Zod 4 involve the *error customization* APIs. They were a bit of a mess in Zod 3; Zod 4 makes things significantly more elegant, to the point where I think it's worth highlighting here.\\n\\nLong story short, there is now a single, unified `error` parameter for customizing errors, replacing the following APIs:\\n\\nReplace `message` with `error` . (The `message` parameter is still supported but deprecated.)\\n\\n```\\n- z.string().min(5, { message: \\\"Too short.\\\" });\\n+ z.string().min(5, { error: \\\"Too short.\\\" });\\n```\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nReplace `invalid_type_error` and `required_error` with `error` (function syntax):\\n\\n```\\n// Zod 3\\n- z.string({ \\n-   required_error: \\\"This field is required\\\" \\n-   invalid_type_error: \\\"Not a string\\\", \\n- });\\n \\n// Zod 4 \\n+ z.string({ error: (issue) => issue.input === undefined ? \\n+  \\\"This field is required\\\" :\\n+  \\\"Not a string\\\" \\n+ });\\n```\\n\\nReplace `errorMap` with `error` (function syntax):\\n\\n```\\n// Zod 3 \\n- z.string({\\n-   errorMap: (issue, ctx) => {\\n-     if (issue.code === \\\"too_small\\\") {\\n-       return { message: `Value must be >${issue.minimum}` };\\n-     }\\n-     return { message: ctx.defaultError };\\n-   },\\n- });\\n \\n// Zod 4\\n+ z.string({\\n+   error: (issue) => {\\n+     if (issue.code === \\\"too_small\\\") {\\n+       return `Value must be >${issue.minimum}`\\n+     }\\n+   },\\n+ });\\n```\\n\\n## [Upgraded `z.discriminatedUnion()`](https://zod.dev/v4?id=upgraded-zdiscriminatedunion)\\n\\nDiscriminated unions now support a number of schema types not previously supported, including unions and pipes:\\n\\n```\\nconst MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n  // simple literal\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"aaa\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n  // union discriminator\\n  z.object({ status: z.union([z.literal(\\\"bbb\\\"), z.literal(\\\"ccc\\\")]) }),\\n  // pipe discriminator\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"fail\\\").transform(val => val.toUpperCase()) }),\\n]);\\n```\\n\\nPerhaps most importantly, discriminated unions now *compose* —you can use one discriminated union as a member of another.\\n\\n```\\nconst BaseError = z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), message: z.string() });\\n \\nconst MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n  z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n  z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"code\\\", [\\n    BaseError.extend({ code: z.literal(400) }),\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n    BaseError.extend({ code: z.literal(401) }),\\n    BaseError.extend({ code: z.literal(500) })\\n  ])\\n]);\\n```\\n\\n## [Multiple values in `z.literal()`](https://zod.dev/v4?id=multiple-values-in-zliteral)\\n\\nThe `z.literal()` API now optionally supports multiple values.\\n\\n```\\nconst httpCodes = z.literal([ 200, 201, 202, 204, 206, 207, 208, 226 ]);\\n \\n// previously in Zod 3:\\nconst httpCodes = z.union([\\n  z.literal(200),\\n  z.literal(201),\\n  z.literal(202),\\n  z.literal(204),\\n  z.literal(206),\\n  z.literal(207),\\n  z.literal(208),\\n  z.literal(226)\\n]);\\n```\\n\\n## [Refinements live inside schemas](https://zod.dev/v4?id=refinements-live-inside-schemas)\\n\\nIn Zod 3, they were stored in a `ZodEffects` class that wrapped the original schema. This was inconvenient, as it meant you couldn't interleave `.refine()` with other schema methods like `.min()` .\\n\\n```\\nz.string()\\n  .refine(val => val.includes(\\\"@\\\"))\\n  .min(5);\\n// ^ ❌ Property 'min' does not exist on type ZodEffects<ZodString, string, string>\\n```\\n\\nIn Zod 4, refinements are stored inside the schemas themselves, so the code above works as expected.\\n\\n```\\nz.string()\\n  .refine(val => val.includes(\\\"@\\\"))\\n  .min(5); // ✅\\n```\\n\\n### [`.overwrite()`](https://zod.dev/v4?id=overwrite)\\n\\nThe `.transform()` method is extremely useful, but it has one major downside: the output type is no longer *introspectable* at runtime. The transform function is a black box that can return anything. This means (among other things) there's no sound way to convert the schema to JSON Schema.\\n\\n```\\nconst Squared = z.number().transform(val => val ** 2);\\n// => ZodPipe<ZodNumber, ZodTransform>\\n```\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nZod 4 introduces a new `.overwrite()` method for representing transforms that *don't change the inferred type* . Unlike `.transform()` , this method returns an instance of the original class. The overwrite function is stored as a refinement, so it doesn't (and can't) modify the inferred type.\\n\\n```\\nz.number().overwrite(val => val ** 2).max(100);\\n// => ZodNumber\\n```\\n\\nThe existing `.trim()` , `.toLowerCase()` and `.toUpperCase()` methods have been reimplemented using `.overwrite()` .\\n\\n## [An extensible foundation: `zod/v4/core`](https://zod.dev/v4?id=an-extensible-foundation-zodv4core)\\n\\nWhile this will not be relevant to the majority of Zod users, it's worth highlighting. The addition of Zod Mini necessitated the creation of a shared sub-package `zod/v4/core` which contains the core functionality shared between Zod and Zod Mini.\\n\\nI was resistant to this at first, but now I see it as one of Zod 4's most important features. It lets Zod level up from a simple library to a fast validation \\\"substrate\\\" that can be sprinkled into other libraries.\\n\\nIf you're building a schema library, refer to the implementations of Zod and Zod Mini to see how to build on top of the foundation `zod/v4/core` provides. Don't hesitate to get in touch in GitHub discussions or via [X](https://x.com/colinhacks) / [Bluesky](https://bsky.app/profile/colinhacks.com) for help or feedback.\\n\\n## [Wrapping up](https://zod.dev/v4?id=wrapping-up)\\n\\nI'm planning to write up a series of additional posts explaining the design process behind some major features like Zod Mini. I'll update this section as those get posted.\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nFor library authors, there is now a dedicated [For library authors](https://zod.dev/library-authors) guide that describes the best practices for building on top of Zod. It answers common questions about how to support Zod 3 & Zod 4 (including Mini) simultaneously.\\n\\n```\\npnpm upgrade zod@latest\\n```\\n\\nHappy parsing!   \\n \\n— Colin McDonnell [@colinhacks](https://x.com/colinhacks)\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Release notes\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/v4#simplified-error-customization\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"## Arrays\\nTo define an array schema:\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const stringArray = z.array(z.string()); // or z.string().array()\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const stringArray = z.array(z.string());\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\nTo access the inner schema for an element of the array.\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        stringArray.unwrap(); // => string schema\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        stringArray.def.element; // => string schema\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\nZod implements a number of array-specific validations:\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        z.array(z.string()).min(5); // must contain 5 or more items\\n        z.array(z.string()).max(5); // must contain 5 or fewer items\\n        z.array(z.string()).length(5); // must contain 5 items exactly\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        z.array(z.string()).check(z.minLength(5)); // must contain 5 or more items\\n        z.array(z.string()).check(z.maxLength(5)); // must contain 5 or fewer items\\n        z.array(z.string()).check(z.length(5)); // must contain 5 items exactly\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\n{/* Unlike `.nonempty()` these methods do not change the inferred type. */}\\n## Tuples\\nUnlike arrays, tuples are typically fixed-length arrays that specify different schemas for each index.\\n```ts\\n    const MyTuple = z.tuple([\\n      z.string(),\\n      z.number(),\\n      z.boolean()\\n    ]);\\n    \\n    type MyTuple = z.infer<typeof MyTuple>;\\n    // [string, number, boolean]\\n```\\nTo add a variadic (\\\"rest\\\") argument:\\n```ts\\n    const variadicTuple = z.tuple([z.string()], z.number());\\n    // => [string, ...number[]];\\n```\\n\\n## Unions\\nUnion types (`A | B`) represent a logical \\\"OR\\\". Zod union schemas will check the input against each option in order. The first value that validates successfully is returned.\\n```ts\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n      z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), error: z.string() }),\\n    ]);\\n```\\nEach option should be an *object schema* whose discriminator prop (`status` in the example above) corresponds to some literal value or set of values, usually `z.enum()`, `z.literal()`, `z.null()`, or `z.undefined()`.\\n{/* \\n  <Callout>\\n  In Zod 3, you were required to specify the discriminator key as the first argument. This is no longer necessary, as Zod can now automatically detect the discriminator key.\\n```ts\\n      const MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n        z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n        z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), error: z.string() }),\\n      ]);\\n  ```\\n  If Zod can't find a discriminator key, it will throw an error at schema creation time.\\n  </Callout> */}\\n<Accordions type=\\\"single\\\">\\n  <Accordion title=\\\"Nesting discriminated unions\\\">\\n    For advanced use cases, discriminated unions can be nested. Zod will figure out the optimal parsing strategy to leverage the discriminators at each level.\\n```ts\\n        const BaseError = { status: z.literal(\\\"failed\\\"), message: z.string() };\\n        const MyErrors = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"code\\\", [\\n          z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(400) }),\\n          z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(401) }),\\n          z.object({ ...BaseError, code: z.literal(500) }),\\n        ]);\\n    \\n        const MyResult = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"status\\\", [\\n          z.object({ status: z.literal(\\\"success\\\"), data: z.string() }),\\n          MyErrors\\n        ]);\\n    ```\\n  </Accordion>\\n</Accordions>\\n\\n## Intersections\\nIntersection types (`A & B`) represent a logical \\\"AND\\\".\\n```ts\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Zod v4 llms-full.txt\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"site\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/llms-full.txt\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nYou're correct—starting in Zod V4, the `discriminator` parameter for `z.discriminatedUnion` is typed as a plain `string`, not as a generic type parameter. This means TypeScript can no longer infer the literal type of the discriminator or its possible values from the API, which reduces type inference precision compared to V3. This change was intentional and made to simplify the implementation, improve performance, and enhance error reporting, as discussed by @colinhacks in several issues ([#4403](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4403), [#4399](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4399), [#4615](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4615)).\\n\\nIf you need to work around this, you can use explicit type assertions (like `Extract`) after checking the discriminator, or define all possible schemas statically before creating the discriminated union to preserve type inference ([discussion](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/discussions/4735)). For runtime introspection of possible discriminator values, you can access the internal `_zod.propValues` property ([example](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4521)), but this isn't reflected in the TypeScript type system.\\n\\nLet me know if you need concrete code examples or further details. If this answers your question, please feel free to close the issue.\\n\\n\\n<!-- Dosu Comment Footer -->\\n*To reply, just mention [@dosu](https://go.dosu.dev/dosubot).*\\n\\n---\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n<sup>How did I do? [Good](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=great_response) | [Irrelevant](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=irrelevant_answer) | [Incorrect](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=incorrect_sources) | [Verbose](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=too_verbose) | [Hallucination](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=hallucination) | [Report 🐛](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=bug_report) | [Other](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/50ac52e3-1eda-44db-995f-fcfad835096c?feedback_type=other)</sup>  [![Chat with Dosu](https://dosu.dev/dosu-chat-badge.svg)](https://app.dosu.dev/74e629f3-4208-4343-87df-d795041fa61a/ask?utm_source=github) [![Join Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/join-5865F2?logo=discord&logoColor=white&label=)](https://go.dosu.dev/discord-bot) [![Share on X](https://img.shields.io/badge/X-share-black)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=%40dosu_ai%20helped%20me%20solve%20this%20issue!&url=https%3A//github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5024)\\n---\\nComment #2 by @joemckie:\\n\\nFWIW, this seems to be working for me (at least, type-wise it throws no errors). Will conduct further testing :)\\n\\n```ts\\nimport { z, type ZodDiscriminatedUnion } from \\\"zod\\\";\\n\\ndeclare module \\\"zod\\\" {\\n  function discriminatedUnion<\\n    D extends string,\\n    Types extends readonly [\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Discriminated union discriminator is no longer generic in V4\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"github_issue\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5024\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n  | z.ZodInvalidUnionIssue // ♻️ renamed to z.core.$ZodIssueInvalidUnion\\n  | z.ZodCustomIssue // ♻️ renamed to z.core.$ZodIssueCustom\\n  | z.ZodInvalidEnumValueIssue // ❌ merged in z.core.$ZodIssueInvalidValue\\n  | z.ZodInvalidLiteralIssue // ❌ merged into z.core.$ZodIssueInvalidValue\\n  | z.ZodInvalidUnionDiscriminatorIssue // ❌ throws an Error at schema creation time\\n  | z.ZodInvalidArgumentsIssue // ❌ z.function throws ZodError directly\\n  | z.ZodInvalidReturnTypeIssue // ❌ z.function throws ZodError directly\\n  | z.ZodInvalidDateIssue // ❌ merged into invalid_type\\n  | z.ZodInvalidIntersectionTypesIssue // ❌ removed (throws regular Error)\\n  | z.ZodNotFiniteIssue // ❌ infinite values no longer accepted (invalid_type)\\n```\\n\\nWhile certain Zod 4 issue types have been merged, dropped, and modified, each issue remains structurally similar to Zod 3 counterpart (identical, in most cases). All issues still conform to the same base interface as Zod 3, so most common error handling logic will work without modification.\\n\\n```\\nexport interface $ZodIssueBase {\\n  readonly code?: string;\\n  readonly input?: unknown;\\n  readonly path: PropertyKey[];\\n  readonly message: string;\\n}\\n```\\n\\n### [changes error map precedence](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=changes-error-map-precedence)\\n\\nThe error map precedence has been changed to be more consistent. Specifically, an error map passed into `.parse()` *no longer* takes precedence over a schema-level error map.\\n\\n```\\nconst mySchema = z.string({ error: () => \\\"Schema-level error\\\" });\\n \\n// in Zod 3\\nmySchema.parse(12, { error: () => \\\"Contextual error\\\" }); // => \\\"Contextual error\\\"\\n \\n// in Zod 4\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Migration guide\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/v4/changelog#znumber\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Title: Zod discriminated union \\\"other string\\\" option\\nCategory: Q&A\\nAuthor: @BrendanC23\\nTimestamp: October 29, 2025\\nBody:\\nI'd like to be able to create a Zod schema like the following:\\r\\n\\r\\n```ts\\r\\nz.discriminatedUnion(\\\"val\\\", [\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.literal(\\\"A\\\"),\\r\\n    a: z.string().min(1),\\r\\n  }),\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.string(),\\r\\n    a: z.string(),\\r\\n  })\\r\\n]);\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nHere, `a` is only required when `val` is `A`. For any other value of `val`, it can be empty. Currently, Zod's discriminated union feature doesn't allow non-literal discriminator values and so `z.string()` cannot be used.\\r\\n\\r\\nI've also tried writing this with a regular `z.union()`. However, an input of `{ val: \\\"A\\\", a: \\\"\\\" }` successfully parses because the second union member is matched (see this [playground link](https://zod-playground.vercel.app?appdata=N4IgzgxgFgpgtgQxALhALwHQFcB2BLAexwAoBtAHRwAIrMCAjAKxggBdjhKaaA3BAG2S0M-PKxgAnAcXIgAgrICUAGi7cEQzGFYS8OAObFFGOHuIBGFWoC%2BV6sIbM2HNbwGaM23QaOr7NDWEvPUM7GltKAF1FAG5KEGUQPn4sGDAUUhBOfyoAcmTcoVy5XL9uPIRCqlkanGsQSMSeSTBCHBQQABYMcx6AJgSQPDAALQIAEwBZPTwUADMBMBhrIA)).\\r\\n\\r\\n```ts\\r\\nz.union([\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.literal(\\\"A\\\"),\\r\\n    a: z.string().min(1),\\r\\n  }),\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.string(),\\r\\n    a: z.string(),\\r\\n  })\\r\\n]);\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nIs there a way to build a schema that can have a `z.string()` option as a fallback for the discriminated union?\\nComments:\\n\\n    Comment #1 from @dosubot on October 29, 2025:\\n\\n    <!-- Answer -->\\nZod's `discriminatedUnion` only supports literal values for the discriminator key, so you can't use `z.string()` as a fallback option in the way you described. This is an intentional design decision for performance and error reporting reasons ([see discussion](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4403), [another discussion](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/3654), [confirmation in v4](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5108)).\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nFor your use case—where `a` is required only when `val` is `\\\"A\\\"`, and otherwise can be empty—there are a few workarounds:\\n\\n**1. Use a custom transform/refinement:**  \\nYou can define a base schema and use `.refine()` or `.superRefine()` to enforce the conditional requirement:\\n\\n```ts\\nconst schema = z.object({\\n  val: z.string(),\\n  a: z.string(),\\n}).superRefine((data, ctx) => {\\n  if (data.val === \\\"A\\\" && !data.a) {\\n    ctx.addIssue({\\n      code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,\\n      message: \\\"`a` is required when `val` is 'A'\\\",\\n      path: [\\\"a\\\"],\\n    });\\n  }\\n});\\n```\\n\\n**2. Use the upcoming `z.switch` API (Zod 4+):**  \\n`z.switch` allows you to select a schema at parse-time based on the input, supporting fallback cases ([see discussion](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/discussions/2109)). Example:\\n\\n```ts\\nconst schema = z.switch((input) => {\\n  if (input.val === \\\"A\\\") {\\n    return z.object({ val: z.literal(\\\"A\\\"), a: z.string().min(1) });\\n  }\\n  return z.object({ val: z.string(), a: z.string() });\\n});\\n```\\n\\n**3. Community workaround with `orFallback`:**  \\nSome users wrap a `discriminatedUnion` with a custom function to provide fallback parsing ([see example](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/discussions/2892)).\\n\\nIf your question is answered, please close the issue! If you need a more tailored example, let me know your Zod version and I can help further.\\n\\n\\n<!-- Dosu Comment Footer -->\\n*To reply, just mention [@dosu](https://go.dosu.dev/dosubot).*\\n\\n---\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n<sup>How did I do? [Good](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=great_response) | [Irrelevant](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=irrelevant_answer) | [Incorrect](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=incorrect_sources) | [Verbose](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=too_verbose) | [Hallucination](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=hallucination) | [Report 🐛](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=bug_report) | [Other](https://app.dosu.dev/response-feedback/ae865bcb-ee5b-4cc2-b1ba-7c73d24e554e?feedback_type=other)</sup>  [![Chat with Dosu](https://dosu.dev/dosu-chat-badge.svg)](https://app.dosu.dev/74e629f3-4208-4343-87df-d795041fa61a/ask?utm_source=github) [![Join Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/join-5865F2?logo=discord&logoColor=white&label=)](https://go.dosu.dev/discord-bot) [![Share on X](https://img.shields.io/badge/X-share-black)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=%40dosu_ai%20helped%20me%20solve%20this%20issue!&url=https%3A//github.com/colinhacks/zod/discussions/5391)\\n\\n    Replies to comment #1:\\n\\n        Reply #1 to comment #1 from dosubot on October 29, 2025:\\n\\n        The `discriminatedUnion` was included in Zod 4 and hasn't been deprecated. The linked issue was closed, and it seems like the experimental `switch` API isn't going to be implemented.\\r\\n\\r\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nThe `orFallback` option is interesting, but it doesn't seem to support a fallback schema, only a literal like `(value) => ({ type: \\\"unknown\\\", value } as const)`.\\n\\n    Comment #2 from @BrendanC23 on October 29, 2025:\\n\\n    It seems like the best option is to do the following:\\r\\n\\r\\n```\\r\\nz.union([\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.literal(\\\"A\\\"),\\r\\n    a: z.string().min(1),\\r\\n  }),\\r\\n  z.object({\\r\\n    val: z.string().refine(str => str !== \\\"A\\\"),\\r\\n    a: z.string(),\\r\\n  })\\r\\n]);\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nThis works, but it isn't ideal because it uses a `z.union()` instead of a `z.discriminatedUnion()` and the `refine()`  call must be kept up-to-date if new union members are added or the existing ones change. It would be great if there was support for non-literal values in discriminated unions, or a straightforward way to designate a fallback schema if none of the discriminated union options are met.\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Zod discriminated union \\\"other string\\\" option\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"github_discussion\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/discussions/5391\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Title: z.discriminatedUnion where the field doesn't exist on one of the options\\nCategory: Q&A\\nAuthor: @GavynWithLayer\\nTimestamp: April 20, 2025\\nBody:\\nIs there any way to have a discriminated union where the discrimination is based on if the field exists or not?\\r\\n\\r\\nFor example:\\r\\n\\r\\n```typescript\\r\\nconst ParameterBase = z.object({\\r\\n    allowEmptyValue: z.optional(z.boolean()).default(false),\\r\\n    deprecated: z.optional(z.boolean()).default(false),\\r\\n    description: z.optional(z.string()),\\r\\n    in: ParameterLocation,\\r\\n    name: z.string(),\\r\\n    required: z.optional(z.boolean()),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nconst Reference = z.object({\\r\\n    '$ref': z.string(),\\r\\n    description: z.optional(z.string()),\\r\\n    summary: z.optional(z.string()),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nz.discriminatedUnion('$ref', [Reference, ParameterBase])\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nI am asking because I want the union to assume it's a `Reference` if the required `$ref` field is present. Otherwise, it should parse as `ParamterBase`. This way, the error messages are more helpful (one of the big benefits of discriminated unions, imo).\\r\\n\\r\\nIf this is not possible, is it possible with `z.custom`? I tried to figure it out for a bit before switching to `z.discriminatedUnion`, but didn't make any progress.\\nComments:\\n\\n    Comment #1 from @colinhacks on April 20, 2025:\\n\\n    You'll need to set the `$ref` key to `z.undefined()`:\\r\\n\\r\\n```ts\\r\\nconst A = z.object({\\r\\n  $ref: z.undefined(),\\r\\n  a: z.string(),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nconst B = z.object({\\r\\n  $ref: z.literal(\\\"asdf\\\"),\\r\\n  b: z.number(),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nconst arg = z.discriminatedUnion(\\\"$ref\\\", [A, B]);\\r\\narg.parse({\\r\\n  a: \\\"asdf\\\",\\r\\n});\\r\\narg.parse({\\r\\n  $ref: \\\"asdf\\\",\\r\\n  b: 123,\\r\\n});\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nThis works in Zod 3 and Zod 4.\\n\\n    Replies to comment #1:\\n\\n        Reply #1 to comment #1 from colinhacks on April 20, 2025:\\n\\n        Does this work if `B`'s `$ref` is not a literal, but any string? I thought I tried this already, to no avail.\\n\\n        Reply #2 to comment #1 from colinhacks on April 21, 2025:\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n        Ah, forgot to mention that part. No, non-literal keys aren't supported in discriminated unions. At that point we're checking the type of the `$ref` key instead of just doing a quick `Map` lookup, watering down the value of the discriminated union optimization. You'll have to use a regular union in this scenario, I'm afraid.\\n\\n        Reply #3 to comment #1 from colinhacks on April 21, 2025:\\n\\n        Thank you for your help!\\n\\n        Reply #4 to comment #1 from colinhacks on April 21, 2025:\\n\\n        I ended up going with something like this:\\r\\n\\r\\n```typescript\\r\\nconst A = z.object({\\r\\n    $ref: z.string(),\\r\\n    a: z.string(),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nconst B = z.object({\\r\\n    b: z.number(),\\r\\n});\\r\\n\\r\\nexport const arg = z.any().transform((value, ctx) => {\\r\\n    if (typeof value !== 'object') {\\r\\n        ctx.addIssue({\\r\\n            code: z.ZodIssueCode.invalid_type,\\r\\n            expected: 'object',\\r\\n            message: `Expected object, received ${typeof value}`,\\r\\n            path: [],\\r\\n            received: typeof value,\\r\\n        });\\r\\n        return z.NEVER;\\r\\n    } else if (value === null) {\\r\\n        ctx.addIssue({\\r\\n            code: z.ZodIssueCode.invalid_type,\\r\\n            expected: 'object',\\r\\n            message: 'Expected object, received null',\\r\\n            path: [],\\r\\n            received: 'null',\\r\\n        });\\r\\n        return z.NEVER;\\r\\n    }\\r\\n\\r\\n    if ('$ref' in value) {\\r\\n        const {data, error, success} = A.safeParse(value);\\r\\n        if (success) {\\r\\n            return data;\\r\\n        } else if (error) {\\r\\n            for (const issue of error.issues) {\\r\\n                ctx.addIssue(issue);\\r\\n            }\\r\\n            return z.NEVER;\\r\\n        }\\r\\n    } else {\\r\\n        const {data, error, success} = B.safeParse(value);\\r\\n        if (success) {\\r\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"            return data;\\r\\n        } else if (error) {\\r\\n            for (const issue of error.issues) {\\r\\n                ctx.addIssue(issue);\\r\\n            }\\r\\n            return z.NEVER;\\r\\n        }\\r\\n    }\\r\\n\\r\\n    const {data, error, success} = z.union([A, B]).safeParse(value);\\r\\n    if (success) {\\r\\n        return data;\\r\\n    } else if (error) {\\r\\n        for (const issue of error.issues) {\\r\\n            ctx.addIssue(issue);\\r\\n        }\\r\\n    }\\r\\n    return z.NEVER;\\r\\n});\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nIt's not great, but it works well enough for what I'm trying to do, I suppose.\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"z.discriminatedUnion where the field doesn't exist on one of the Issue by @de-don created on December 02, 2025. The current state of the issue is: closed\\n\\n```\\nexport const example = z.union([\\n  z.string().min(10),\\n  z.string().min(5)\\n]);\\n```\\n\\nwill generate schema like this in `zod@4.1.13`\\n\\n```\\n\\\"exampleField\\\": {\\n  \\\"anyOf\\\":[\\n    {\\\"type\\\":\\\"string\\\",\\\"minLength\\\":10},\\n    {\\\"type\\\":\\\"string\\\",\\\"minLength\\\":5}\\n  ]\\n}\\n```\\n\\nbut in zod v3 it generates:\\n```\\n\\\"exampleField\\\": {\\n  \\\"oneOf\\\":[\\n    {\\\"type\\\":\\\"string\\\",\\\"minLength\\\":10},\\n    {\\\"type\\\":\\\"string\\\",\\\"minLength\\\":5}\\n  ]\\n}\\n```\\n\\nI think it should use `oneOf` instead of `anyOf`\\n---\\nComment #1 by @coderabbitai[bot]:\\n\\n<!-- This is an auto-generated issue plan by CodeRabbit -->\\n\\n\\n## Duplicate Detection\\nℹ️ This issue may be similar to colinhacks/zod#5493.\\n\\n\\n## Related Issues\\n- colinhacks/zod#5493: Union type generates anyOf instead oneOf\\n\\n\\n## Suggested Issue Assignees\\n- ddontsov-b2broker\\n\\n\\n## Actions\\n- [ ] <!-- {\\\"checkboxId\\\": \\\"8d4f2b9c-3e1a-4f7c-a9b2-d5e8f1c4a7b9\\\"} --> 📝 Generate an implementation plan for this issue\\n\\n---\\n<sub>ℹ️ **Note:** Issue enrichment is currently in early access.</sub>\\n\\n<details>\\n<summary>Disable automatic issue enrichment</summary>\\n\\n<sub>To disable automatic issue enrichment, add the following to your `.coderabbit.yaml`:\\n```yaml\\nissue_enrichment:\\n  auto_enrich:\\n    enabled: false\\n```\\n</sub>\\n</details>\\n---\\nComment #2 by @de-don:\\n\\nI just recreated this from another account, and closed previous issue, sorry. This is a right one\\n---\\nComment #3 by @dosubot[bot]:\\n\\n<!-- Greeting -->\\nHi @de-don! I'm [Dosu](https://go.dosu.dev/dosubot) and I’m helping the zod team.\\n\\n<!-- Answer -->\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nThis change in Zod v4 is intentional: unions now generate JSON Schema using `anyOf` instead of `oneOf` because Zod unions are non-exclusive—multiple schemas can match the same value—so `anyOf` is the correct representation according to the JSON Schema spec. In contrast, `oneOf` requires that exactly one schema matches, which is only guaranteed for discriminated unions (where a specific field distinguishes each variant). For discriminated unions, Zod v4 does use `oneOf` as expected. You can see the rationale and implementation details in [this merged PR](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/pull/5453) and related [issue discussion](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/4089).\\n\\nThere’s no built-in config to switch regular unions back to `oneOf`, but you can use the `override` option in `z.toJSONSchema()` to manually adjust the output. For example:\\n\\n```ts\\nz.toJSONSchema(schema, {\\n  override(ctx) {\\n    if (ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf) {\\n      ctx.jsonSchema.oneOf = ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf;\\n      delete ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf;\\n    }\\n  }\\n});\\n```\\n\\nIf this answers your question, feel free to close the issue! If you need more details or have follow-ups, let me know.\\n\\n\\n<!-- Dosu Comment Footer -->\\n*To reply, just mention [@dosu](https://go.dosu.dev/dosubot).*\\n\\n---\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n<sup>How did I do? #4 by @de-don:\\n\\n@dosubot, how should I proceed with the case, when I need union from string and int?\\n---\\nComment #5 by @dosubot[bot]:\\n\\n<!-- Answer -->\\nFor a union of string and int in Zod v4, use `z.union([z.string(), z.number().int()])`. When you convert this to JSON Schema with `z.toJSONSchema()`, Zod will generate an `anyOf` array, not `oneOf`. This is intentional—Zod unions are non-exclusive, so `anyOf` matches the semantics of Zod unions and the JSON Schema spec. `oneOf` is only used for discriminated unions, where exactly one schema matches due to a discriminator field. You can see this logic in the [Zod codebase](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/blob/6e968a3b49cbcb3bffc30c68634e80168e8f2a2e/packages/zod/src/v4/core/to-json-schema.ts#L331-L347) and in [issue discussions](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5493#issuecomment-3601343212).\\n\\nIf you need the output to use `oneOf` instead, you can post-process the schema using the `override` option:\\n\\n```ts\\nconst schema = z.union([z.string(), z.number().int()]);\\nconst jsonSchema = z.toJSONSchema(schema, {\\n  override(ctx) {\\n    if (ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf) {\\n      ctx.jsonSchema.oneOf = ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf;\\n      delete ctx.jsonSchema.anyOf;\\n    }\\n  }\\n});\\n```\\n\\nMore details are in the [Zod JSON Schema docs](https://zod.dev/json-schema) and [related issues](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5493#issuecomment-3601343212). "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"## [Refinements](https://zod.dev/api?id=refinements)\\n\\nEvery Zod schema stores an array of *refinements* . Refinements are a way to perform custom validation that Zod doesn't provide a native API for.\\n\\n### [`.refine()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=refine)\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().check(z.refine((val) => val.length <= 255));\\n```\\n\\n   Refinement functions should never throw. Instead they should return a falsy value to signal failure. Thrown errors are not caught by Zod.\\n\\n#### [`error`](https://zod.dev/api?id=error)\\n\\nTo customize the error message:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().check(\\n  z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" })\\n);\\n```\\n\\n#### [`abort`](https://zod.dev/api?id=abort)\\n\\nBy default, validation issues from checks are considered *continuable* ; that is, Zod will execute *all* checks in sequence, even if one of them causes a validation error. This is usually desirable, as it means Zod can surface as many errors as possible in one go.\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().check(\\n  z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" }),\\n  z.refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" })\\n);\\n \\nconst result = z.safeParse(myString, \\\"OH NO\\\");\\nresult.error?.issues;\\n/* [\\n  { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" },\\n  { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" }\\n] */\\n```\\n\\nTo mark a particular refinement as *non-continuable* , use the `abort` parameter. Validation will terminate if the check fails.\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().check(\\n  z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\", abort: true }),\\n  z.refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\", abort: true })\\n);\\n \\nconst result = z.safeParse(myString, \\\"OH NO\\\");\\nresult.error?.issues;\\n// [ { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" }]\\n```\\n\\n#### [`path`](https://zod.dev/api?id=path)\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nTo customize the error path, use the `path` parameter. This is typically only useful in the context of object schemas.\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst passwordForm = z\\n  .object({\\n    password: z.string(),\\n    confirm: z.string(),\\n  })\\n  .check(z.refine((data) => data.password === data.confirm, {\\n    message: \\\"Passwords don't match\\\",\\n    path: [\\\"confirm\\\"], // path of error\\n  }));\\n```\\n\\nThis will set the `path` parameter in the associated issue:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst result = z.safeParse(passwordForm, { password: \\\"asdf\\\", confirm: \\\"qwer\\\" });\\nresult.error.issues;\\n/* [{\\n  \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\",\\n  \\\"path\\\": [ \\\"confirm\\\" ],\\n  \\\"message\\\": \\\"Passwords don't match\\\"\\n}] */\\n```\\n\\nTo define an asynchronous refinement, just pass an `async` function:\\n\\n```\\nconst userId = z.string().refine(async (id) => {\\n  // verify that ID exists in database\\n  return true;\\n});\\n```\\n\\n   If you use async refinements, you must use the `.parseAsync` method to parse data! Otherwise Zod will throw an error.\\n\\n Zod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst result = await z.parseAsync(userId, \\\"abc123\\\");\\n```\\n\\n#### [`when`](https://zod.dev/api?id=when)\\n\\n**Note** — This is a power user feature and can absolutely be abused in ways that will increase the probability of uncaught errors originating from inside your refinements.\\n\\nBy default, refinements don't run if any *non-continuable* issues have already been encountered. Zod is careful to ensure the type signature of the value is correct before passing it into any refinement functions.\\n\\n```\\nconst schema = z.string().refine((val) => {\\n  return val.length > 8\\n});\\n \\nschema.parse(1234); // invalid_type: refinement won't be executed\\n```\\n\\nIn some cases, you want finer control over when refinements run. For instance consider this \\\"password confirm\\\" check:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nconst schema = z\\n  .object({\\n    password: z.string().check(z.minLength(8)),\\n    confirmPassword: z.string(),\\n    anotherField: z.string(),\\n  })\\n  .check(z.refine((data) => data.password === data.confirmPassword, {\\n    message: \\\"Passwords do not match\\\",\\n    path: [\\\"confirmPassword\\\"],\\n  }));\\n \\nschema.parse({\\n  password: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  confirmPassword: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  anotherField: 1234 // ❌ this error will prevent the password check from running\\n});\\n```\\n\\nAn error on `anotherField` will prevent the password confirmation check from executing, even though the check doesn't depend on `anotherField` . To control when a refinement will run, use the `when` parameter:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst schema = z\\n  .object({\\n    password: z.string().min(8),\\n    confirmPassword: z.string(),\\n    anotherField: z.string(),\\n  })\\n  .check(z.refine((data) => data.password === data.confirmPassword, {\\n    message: \\\"Passwords do not match\\\",\\n    path: [\\\"confirmPassword\\\"],\\n    when(payload) { \\n      // no issues with `password` or `confirmPassword`\\n      return payload.issues.every((iss) => { \\n        const firstPathEl = iss.path?.[0]; \\n        return firstPathEl !== \\\"password\\\" && firstPathEl !== \\\"confirmPassword\\\"; \\n      }); \\n    },  \\n  }));\\n \\nschema.parse({\\n  password: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  confirmPassword: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  anotherField: 1234 // ❌ this error will prevent the password check from running\\n});\\n```\\n\\n### [`.superRefine()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=superrefine)\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"The regular `.refine` API only generates a single issue with a `\\\"custom\\\"` error code, but `.superRefine()` makes it possible to create multiple issues using any of Zod's [internal issue types](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/blob/main/packages/zod/src/v4/core/errors.ts) .\\n\\nZod Zod Mini     \\n```\\nconst UniqueStringArray = z.array(z.string()).check(\\n  z.superRefine((val, ctx) => {\\n    if (val.length > 3) {\\n      ctx.addIssue({\\n        code: \\\"too_big\\\",\\n        maximum: 3,\\n        origin: \\\"array\\\",\\n        inclusive: true,\\n        message: \\\"Too many items 😡\\\",\\n        input: val,\\n      });\\n    }\\n \\n    if (val.length !== new Set(val).size) {\\n      ctx.addIssue({\\n        code: \\\"custom\\\",\\n        message: `No duplicates allowed.`,\\n        input: val,\\n      });\\n    }\\n  })\\n);\\n```\\n\\n### [`.check()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=check)\\n\\n**Note** — The `.check()` API is a more low-level API that's generally more complex than `.superRefine()` . It can be faster in performance-sensitive code paths, but it's also more verbose.\\n\\n###  View example\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Defining schemas\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/api#refinements\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"## [Refinements](https://zod.dev/api?id=refinements)\\n\\nEvery Zod schema stores an array of *refinements* . Refinements are a way to perform custom validation that Zod doesn't provide a native API for.\\n\\n### [`.refine()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=refine)\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().refine((val) => val.length <= 255);\\n```\\n \\n   Refinement functions should never throw. Instead they should return a falsy value to signal failure. Thrown errors are not caught by Zod.\\n\\n#### [`error`](https://zod.dev/api?id=error)\\n\\nTo customize the error message:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string().refine((val) => val.length > 8, { \\n  error: \\\"Too short!\\\" \\n});\\n```\\n \\n#### [`abort`](https://zod.dev/api?id=abort)\\n\\nBy default, validation issues from checks are considered *continuable* ; that is, Zod will execute *all* checks in sequence, even if one of them causes a validation error. This is usually desirable, as it means Zod can surface as many errors as possible in one go.\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string()\\n  .refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" })\\n  .refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" });\\n\\nconst result = myString.safeParse(\\\"OH NO\\\");\\nresult.error?.issues;\\n/* [\\n  { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" },\\n  { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" }\\n] */\\n```\\n \\nTo mark a particular refinement as *non-continuable* , use the `abort` parameter. Validation will terminate if the check fails.\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst myString = z.string()\\n  .refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\", abort: true })\\n  .refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\", abort: true });\\n\\nconst result = myString.safeParse(\\\"OH NO\\\");\\nresult.error?.issues;\\n// => [{ \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" }]\\n```\\n \\n#### [`path`](https://zod.dev/api?id=path)\\n\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n    password: z.string().min(8),\\n    confirmPassword: z.string(),\\n    anotherField: z.string(),\\n  })\\n  .refine((data) => data.password === data.confirmPassword, {\\n    message: \\\"Passwords do not match\\\",\\n    path: [\\\"confirmPassword\\\"],\\n  });\\n \\nschema.parse({\\n  password: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  confirmPassword: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  anotherField: 1234 // ❌ this error will prevent the password check from running\\n});\\n```\\n \\nAn error on `anotherField` will prevent the password confirmation check from executing, even though the check doesn't depend on `anotherField` . To control when a refinement will run, use the `when` parameter:\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst schema = z\\n  .object({\\n    password: z.string().min(8),\\n    confirmPassword: z.string(),\\n    anotherField: z.string(),\\n  })\\n  .refine((data) => data.password === data.confirmPassword, {\\n    message: \\\"Passwords do not match\\\",\\n    path: [\\\"confirmPassword\\\"],\\n \\n    // run if password & confirmPassword are valid\\n    when(payload) { \\n      return schema \\n        .pick({ password: true, confirmPassword: true }) \\n        .safeParse(payload.value).success; \\n    },  \\n  });\\n \\nschema.parse({\\n  password: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  confirmPassword: \\\"asdf\\\",\\n  anotherField: 1234 // ❌ this error will not prevent the password check from running\\n});\\n```\\n \\n### [`.superRefine()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=superrefine)\\n\\nThe regular `.refine` API only generates a single issue with a `\\\"custom\\\"` error code, but `.superRefine()` makes it possible to create multiple issues using any of Zod's [internal issue types](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/blob/main/packages/zod/src/v4/core/errors.ts) .\\n\\nZod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst UniqueStringArray = z.array(z.string()).superRefine((val, ctx) => {\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n  if (val.length > 3) {\\n    ctx.addIssue({\\n      code: \\\"too_big\\\",\\n      maximum: 3,\\n      origin: \\\"array\\\",\\n      inclusive: true,\\n      message: \\\"Too many items 😡\\\",\\n      input: val,\\n    });\\n  }\\n \\n  if (val.length !== new Set(val).size) {\\n    ctx.addIssue({\\n      code: \\\"custom\\\",\\n      message: `No duplicates allowed.`,\\n      input: val,\\n    });\\n  }\\n});\\n \\n```\\n \\n### [`.check()`](https://zod.dev/api?id=check)\\n\\n**Note** — The `.check()` API is a more low-level API that's generally more complex than `.superRefine()` . It can be faster in performance-sensitive code paths, but it's also more verbose.\\n\\n###  View example\\n\\n The `.refine()` API is syntactic sugar atop a more versatile (and verbose) API called `.check()` . You can use this API to create multiple issues in a single refinement or have full control of the generated issue objects.\\n\\n Zod Zod Mini    \\n```\\nconst UniqueStringArray = z.array(z.string()).check((ctx) => {\\n  if (ctx.value.length > 3) {\\n    // full control of issue objects\\n    ctx.issues.push({\\n      code: \\\"too_big\\\",\\n      maximum: 3,\\n      origin: \\\"array\\\",\\n      inclusive: true,\\n      message: \\\"Too many items 😡\\\",\\n      input: ctx.value\\n    });\\n  }\\n \\n  // create multiple issues in one refinement\\n  if (ctx.value.length !== new Set(ctx.value).size) {\\n    ctx.issues.push({\\n      code: \\\"custom\\\",\\n      message: `No duplicates allowed.`,\\n      input: ctx.value,\\n      continue: true // make this issue continuable (default: false)\\n    });\\n  }\\n});\\n```\\n\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Defining schemas\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/api#refinements\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\nconst mySchema = z.unknown().refine((val): val is string => {\\n  return typeof val === \\\"string\\\"\\n});\\n \\ntype MySchema = z.infer<typeof mySchema>; \\n// Zod 3: `string`\\n// Zod 4: still `unknown`\\n```\\n\\n### [drops `ctx.path`](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=drops-ctxpath)\\n\\nZod's new parsing architecture does not eagerly evaluate the `path` array. This was a necessary change that unlocks Zod 4's dramatic performance improvements.\\n\\n```\\nz.string().superRefine((val, ctx) => {\\n  ctx.path; // ❌ no longer available\\n});\\n```\\n\\n### [drops function as second argument](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=drops-function-as-second-argument)\\n\\nThe following horrifying overload has been removed.\\n\\n```\\nconst longString = z.string().refine(\\n  (val) => val.length > 10,\\n  (val) => ({ message: `${val} is not more than 10 characters` })\\n);\\n```\\n\\n## [`z.ostring()` , etc dropped](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=zostring-etc-dropped)\\n\\nThe undocumented convenience methods `z.ostring()` , `z.onumber()` , etc. have been removed. These were shorthand methods for defining optional string schemas.\\n\\n## [`z.literal()`](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=zliteral)\\n\\n### [drops `symbol` support](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=drops-symbol-support)\\n\\nSymbols aren't considered literal values, nor can they be simply compared with `===` . This was an oversight in Zod 3.\\n\\n## [static `.create()` factories dropped](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=static-create-factories-dropped)\\n\\nPreviously all Zod classes defined a static `.create()` method. These are now implemented as standalone factory functions.\\n\\n```\\nz.ZodString.create(); // ❌ \\n```\\n\\n## [`z.record()`](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=zrecord)\\n\\n### [drops single argument usage](https://zod.dev/v4/changelog?id=drops-single-argument-usage)\\n\\nBefore, `z.record()` could be used with a single argument. This is no longer supported.\\n\\n```\\n// Zod 3\\nz.record(z.string()); // ✅\\n \\n// Zod 4\\nz.record(z.string()); // ❌\"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Migration guide\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/v4/changelog#zarray\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Zod emphasizes *completeness* and *correctness* in its error reporting. In many cases, it's helpful to convert the `$ZodError` to a more useful format. Zod provides some utilities for this.\\n\\nConsider this simple object schema.\\n\\n```\\nimport * as z from \\\"zod\\\";\\n \\nconst schema = z.strictObject({\\n  username: z.string(),\\n  favoriteNumbers: z.array(z.number()),\\n});\\n```\\n\\nAttempting to parse this invalid data results in an error containing three issues.\\n\\n```\\nconst result = schema.safeParse({\\n  username: 1234,\\n  favoriteNumbers: [1234, \\\"4567\\\"],\\n  extraKey: 1234,\\n});\\n \\nresult.error!.issues;\\n[\\n  {\\n    expected: 'string',\\n    code: 'invalid_type',\\n    path: [ 'username' ],\\n    message: 'Invalid input: expected string, received number'\\n  },\\n  {\\n    expected: 'number',\\n    code: 'invalid_type',\\n    path: [ 'favoriteNumbers', 1 ],\\n    message: 'Invalid input: expected number, received string'\\n  },\\n  {\\n    code: 'unrecognized_keys',\\n    keys: [ 'extraKey' ],\\n    path: [],\\n    message: 'Unrecognized key: \\\"extraKey\\\"'\\n  }\\n];\\n```\\n\\n## [`z.treeifyError()`](https://zod.dev/error-formatting?id=ztreeifyerror)\\n\\nTo convert (\\\"treeify\\\") this error into a nested object, use `z.treeifyError()` .\\n\\n```\\nconst tree = z.treeifyError(result.error);\\n \\n// =>\\n{\\n  errors: [ 'Unrecognized key: \\\"extraKey\\\"' ],\\n  properties: {\\n    username: { errors: [ 'Invalid input: expected string, received number' ] },\\n    favoriteNumbers: {\\n      errors: [],\\n      items: [\\n        undefined,\\n        {\\n          errors: [ 'Invalid input: expected number, received string' ]\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\nThe result is a nested structure that mirrors the schema itself. You can easily access the errors that occurred at a particular path. The `errors` field contains the error messages at a given path, and the special properties `properties` and `items` let you traverse deeper into the tree.\\n\\n```\\ntree.properties?.username?.errors;\\n// => [\\\"Invalid input: expected string, received number\\\"]\\n \"}],\"type\":\"content\"},\"title\":\"Formatting errors\",\"context\":null,\"record_type\":\"documentation\",\"url\":\"https://zod.dev/error-formatting#zflattenerror\"},{\"type\":\"document\",\"source\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"## Refinements\\nEvery Zod schema stores an array of *refinements*. Refinements are a way to perform custom validation that Zod doesn't provide a native API for.\\n### `.refine()`\\n{/* <Callout>\\n  Checks do not (in fact, cannot) change the inferred type of the schema.\\n  </Callout>\\n  ### `.refine()` */}\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().refine((val) => val.length <= 255);\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().check(z.refine((val) => val.length <= 255));\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\n<Callout type=\\\"warn\\\">\\n  Refinement functions should never throw. Instead they should return a falsy value to signal failure. Thrown errors are not caught by Zod.\\n</Callout>\\n#### `error`\\nTo customize the error message:\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().refine((val) => val.length > 8, { \\n          error: \\\"Too short!\\\" \\n        });\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().check(\\n          z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" })\\n        );\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\n#### `abort`\\nBy default, validation issues from checks are considered *continuable*; that is, Zod will execute *all* checks in sequence, even if one of them causes a validation error. This is usually desirable, as it means Zod can surface as many errors as possible in one go.\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string()\\n          .refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" })\\n          .refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" });\\n          \\n    \\n        const result = myString.safeParse(\\\"OH NO\\\");\\n        result.error?.issues;\\n        /* [\\n          { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" },\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n          { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" }\\n        ] */\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().check(\\n          z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\" }),\\n          z.refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" })\\n        );\\n    \\n        const result = z.safeParse(myString, \\\"OH NO\\\");\\n        result.error?.issues;\\n        /* [\\n          { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" },\\n          { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Must be lowercase\\\" }\\n        ] */\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\nTo mark a particular refinement as *non-continuable*, use the `abort` parameter. Validation will terminate if the check fails.\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string()\\n          .refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\", abort: true })\\n          .refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\", abort: true });\\n    \\n    \\n        const result = myString.safeParse(\\\"OH NO\\\");\\n        result.error?.issues;\\n        // => [{ \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" }]\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const myString = z.string().check(\\n          z.refine((val) => val.length > 8, { error: \\\"Too short!\\\", abort: true }),\\n          z.refine((val) => val === val.toLowerCase(), { error: \\\"Must be lowercase\\\", abort: true })\\n        );\\n    \\n        const result = z.safeParse(myString, \\\"OH NO\\\");\\n        result.error?.issues;\\n        // [ { \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"Too short!\\\" }]\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\n#### `path`\\nTo customize the error path, use the `path` parameter. This is typically only useful in the context of object schemas.\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const passwordForm = z\\n          .object({\\n            password: z.string(),\\n            confirm: z.string(),\\n          })\\n          .refine((data) => data.password === data.confirm, {\\n            message: \\\"Passwords don't match\\\",\\n            path: [\\\"confirm\\\"], // path of error\\n          });\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const passwordForm = z\\n          .object({\\n            password: z.string(),\\n            confirm: z.string(),\\n          })\\n          .check(z.refine((data) => data.password === data.confirm, {\\n            message: \\\"Passwords don't match\\\",\\n            path: [\\\"confirm\\\"], // path of error\\n          }));\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\nThis will set the `path` parameter in the associated issue:\\n<Tabs groupId=\\\"lib\\\" items={[\\\"Zod\\\", \\\"Zod Mini\\\"]}>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const result = passwordForm.safeParse({ password: \\\"asdf\\\", confirm: \\\"qwer\\\" });\\n        result.error.issues;\\n        /* [{\\n          \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\",\\n          \\\"path\\\": [ \\\"confirm\\\" ],\\n          \\\"message\\\": \\\"Passwords don't match\\\"\\n        }] */\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n  <Tab value=\\\"Zod Mini\\\">\\n```ts\\n        const result = z.safeParse(passwordForm, { password: \\\"asdf\\\", confirm: \\\"qwer\\\" });\\n        result.error.issues;\\n        /* [{\\n          \\\"code\\\": \\\"custom\\\",\\n          \\\"path\\\": [ \\\"confirm\\\" ],\\n          \\\"message\\\": \\\"Passwords don't match\\\"\\n        }] */\\n    ```\\n  </Tab>\\n</Tabs>\\nTo define an asynchronous refinement, just pass an `async` function:\\n```ts\\n    const userId = z.string().refine(async (id) => {\\n      // verify that ID exists in database\\n      return true;\\n    });\\n```\\n<Callout>\"},{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"\\n              code: \\\"too_big\\\",\\n              maximum: 3,\\n              origin: \\\"array\\\",\\n              inclusive: true,\\n
